other_relevant_acts: Equal Opportunity (Religious Exceptions) Amendment Act 2021 (Vic) - narrowed religious exemptions
Health Safeguards for People Born with Variations in Sex Characteristics Act 2025 (Vic) - protects intersex children from unnecessary medical interventions (passed 19 February 2026)

sexual_orientation.definition_in_act: Sexual orientation means a person's emotional, affectional and sexual attraction to, or intimate or sexual relations with, persons of a different gender or the same gender or more than one gender

gender_identity.definition_in_act: Gender identity means a person's gender-related identity, appearance or mannerisms or other gender-related characteristics of the person, whether by way of medical intervention or not, with or without regard to the person's designated sex at birth
gender_identity.source: Equal Opportunity Act 2010 (Vic), s 4 (updated definition effective 20 September 2025)
gender_identity.notes: Definition updated in 2025 to be more inclusive of non-binary and gender diverse people

intersex_status_or_sex_characteristics.definition_in_act: Sex characteristics means a person's physical features relating to sex, including: (a) chromosomes; (b) genes; (c) hormones; (d) genitals; (e) other physical features, whether or not the person has undergone medical intervention
intersex_status_or_sex_characteristics.notes: Victoria added 'sex characteristics' in February 2021, replacing the need for 'intersex status' protection

medical_safeguards.act: Health Safeguards for People Born with Variations in Sex Characteristics Act 2025 (Vic)
key_provisions: Restricts permanent or hard-to-reverse medical treatments altering sex characteristics of intersex children or adults unable to consent
Establishes independent expert panel comprising individuals with lived experience and specialists in medicine, human rights, ethics, mental health, and law
Panel oversees medical treatment plans for intersex children and others lacking capacity to consent
Provides additional support mechanisms for parents and clinicians
Preserves parental/caregiver consent for medically necessary treatments
medical_safeguards.significance: Victoria is the first Australian state and second jurisdiction (after the ACT) to enshrine protections against unnecessary medical interventions on intersex children. Joins a handful of countries worldwide with such protections.

religious_organisations.summary: Religious exemptions were significantly narrowed in 2021. Religious organisations can now only discriminate on the basis of religious belief (not sexual orientation or gender identity) in: (1) employment in religious schools; (2) student selection in religious schools; and (3) government-funded service provision. Discrimination on grounds of sexual orientation and gender identity is still permitted in non-government-funded service provision by religious organisations.
religious_organisations.source: Equal Opportunity Act 2010 (Vic), s 82, s 83, s 84 (as amended by Equal Opportunity (Religious Exceptions) Amendment Act 2021)

small_employers.summary: No small employer exemption in Victoria

other_exemptions.summary: Discrimination on the ground of gender identity or sex characteristics may be permitted in competitive sporting activities in certain circumstances

complaint_body.name: Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (VCAT) / Victorian Equal Opportunity and Human Rights Commission (VEOHRC)
complaint_body.time_limit: 12 months from the act of discrimination (VEOHRC can assist with dispute resolution; formal complaints go to VCAT)
complaint_body.costs_summary: No fee for lodging a discrimination complaint with VCAT

statutory_basis: Racial and Religious Tolerance Act 2001 (Vic) - race and religion only
vilification.notes: Victoria has vilification laws for race and religion only. There are NO vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people as at 31 December 2025, despite long-standing promises by the Victorian Government to introduce these protections. A parliamentary inquiry previously investigated this issue but reforms have not been implemented.
vilification.source: Racial and Religious Tolerance Act 2001 (Vic); Alastair Lawrie analysis

how_to_contact: Call 131 444 (Police Assistance Line) and ask for an LGBTIQA+ Liaison Officer
Visit your local police station and request an LLO
police_reporting.source: Victoria Police website - over 450 LLOs across Victoria

support_services.name: Victorian Equal Opportunity and Human Rights Commission
support_services.role: Rights advice, dispute resolution & discrimination information

key_points_summary: Sexual orientation, gender identity and sex characteristics are all protected attributes (strongest protections in Australia)
Historic intersex medical safeguards legislation passed 19 February 2026 - first Australian state to protect intersex children from unnecessary medical interventions

Religious exemptions significantly narrowed in 2021 - religious schools can only discriminate on basis of religious belief, not sexual orientation or gender identity, in employment and student selection
Religious organisations can still discriminate on grounds of sexual orientation and gender identity in non-government-funded service provision
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people (only race and religion protected)
Positive duty on organisations to prevent discrimination and sexual harassment (introduced 2025)

sexual_orientation.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against because you are gay, lesbian, bisexual, or any other sexual orientation.
real_world_examples: Your employer cannot fire you for being gay
A landlord cannot refuse to rent to you because you're in a same-sex relationship
A hospital cannot deny you treatment based on sexual orientation
A school cannot expel your child for having same-sex parents
A café cannot refuse to serve you because you're in a same-sex relationship
sexual_orientation.limitations: None - Victoria has comprehensive protections for sexual orientation since 2010.

gender_identity.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against based on your gender identity, including if you are transgender, non-binary, genderfluid, or agender.
real_world_examples: Your employer must use your correct name and pronouns
You cannot be denied employment because you are transitioning
A landlord cannot refuse housing because you are trans or non-binary
Schools cannot prevent you from using facilities matching your gender identity
You cannot be refused service at a shop, restaurant, or doctor because of your gender identity
A religious school cannot refuse to hire you as a teacher just because you're trans
gender_identity.limitations: None - Victoria updated the definition in September 2025 to be more inclusive of non-binary and gender diverse people. Religious schools reformed in 2021 can only discriminate based on religious belief, not gender identity.

sex_characteristics.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against based on physical sex characteristics (chromosomes, hormones, anatomy). Additionally, intersex children are now protected from unnecessary medical interventions under the Health Safeguards for People Born with Variations in Sex Characteristics Act 2025.
real_world_examples: You cannot be denied medical care based on being intersex
An employer cannot ask invasive questions about your body
You cannot be excluded from school activities because of sex characteristics
A gym cannot refuse you membership because you are intersex
Intersex children cannot be subjected to unnecessary medical procedures to alter their sex characteristics without their consent (from 2026)
An independent expert panel must oversee treatment plans for intersex children
sex_characteristics.limitations: None - Victoria added sex characteristics protection in February 2021 and passed historic intersex medical safeguards in February 2026, making it the strongest protection in Australia.

what_youre_protected_from: Being rejected for a job because of sexual orientation/gender identity
Being paid less than colleagues because you're LGBTIQ+
Being harassed by coworkers or managers
Being forced to hide your identity at work
Being fired because you transition or come out
Religious schools refusing to hire you just because you're LGBTIQ+ (they can only discriminate based on religious belief)
not_protected_from: Performance-based discipline (if you're underperforming for legitimate work reasons)

what_youre_protected_from: Schools refusing to enroll your child because you're a same-sex couple
Universities discriminating in admissions
Teachers intentionally using wrong pronouns
Being excluded from school events, sports, or facilities
Disciplinary action for being LGBTIQ+
Religious schools expelling students for being LGBTIQ+
Landlords refusing to rent because you're in a same-sex relationship
Being evicted because of your gender identity
Real estate agents refusing to show you properties
Being denied mortgage approval because you're LGBTIQ+
Being refused service at a café or restaurant
A doctor refusing to treat you
Being denied access to public facilities
Hotels refusing to book rooms for same-sex couples
Hairdressers refusing service
Government-funded religious services refusing to serve you
not_protected_from: Non-government-funded religious services (they can still discriminate on grounds of sexual orientation and gender identity)

what_youre_protected_from: Being excluded from a sports team because of sexual orientation
Being denied membership in a professional association
Being expelled from a club

religious_organisations.plain_english_summary: Victoria significantly narrowed religious exemptions in 2021. Religious schools can now only discriminate based on religious belief, NOT sexual orientation or gender identity.
what_they_can_do: Religious schools can require staff and students to uphold religious beliefs
Religious organisations can discriminate in non-government-funded service provision on grounds of sexual orientation and gender identity
what_they_cannot_do: Religious schools cannot refuse to hire someone just for being gay, lesbian, bisexual, or trans
Religious schools cannot expel students for being LGBTIQ+
Government-funded religious services cannot discriminate against LGBTIQ+ service users
religious_organisations.real_example: A Catholic school can require teachers to attend Mass and uphold Catholic teachings, but cannot refuse to hire a teacher simply because they are gay or transgender. The school can only make employment decisions based on religious belief, not LGBTIQ+ status.
religious_organisations.if_you_face_discrimination: Victoria has some of the strongest protections in Australia. If a religious organisation claims they can discriminate against you, they must prove it's based on religious belief, not LGBTIQ+ status. Seek legal advice from VEOHRC.

jurisdiction_rating.rating_explanation: Victoria has strong protections for LGBTIQ+ people with comprehensive coverage of sexual orientation, gender identity, and sex characteristics. Religious exemptions were significantly narrowed in 2021. However, Victoria lacks vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people despite long-standing promises.
strengths: Sexual orientation, gender identity, and sex characteristics all protected (strongest in Australia)
First Australian state to legislate intersex medical safeguards (Health Safeguards for People Born with Variations in Sex Characteristics Act 2025, passed February 2026)
Religious exemptions significantly narrowed in 2021 - religious schools can only discriminate based on religious belief, not LGBTIQ+ status
Positive duty on organisations to prevent discrimination (introduced 2025)
gaps: No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people (only race and religion protected)
Religious organisations can still discriminate in non-government-funded service provision
Victorian Government has promised vilification protections for years but not delivered

primary_legislation: Health Safeguards for People Born with Variations in Sex Characteristics Act 2025 (Vic) - https://www.legislation.vic.gov.au/bills/health-safeguards-people-born-variations-sex-characteristics-bill-2025
official_guides: Victorian Equal Opportunity and Human Rights Commission - https://www.humanrights.vic.gov.au
secondary_analysis: Alastair Lawrie - A Quick Guide to Australian LGBTI Anti-Discrimination Laws
